question:
What is the units (i.e., rightmost) digit of
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{10^{20000}}{10^{100}+3}\right\rfloor ?
\]
%Here $\lfloor x \rfloor$ is the greatest integer less than or equal to
%$x$.
solution:
Solution. Taking \( x=10^{100} \) and \( y=-3 \) in the factorization
\[
x^{200}-y^{200}=(x-y)\left(x^{199}+x^{198} y+\cdots+x y^{198}+y^{199}\right)
\]
shows that the number
\[
I=\frac{10^{20000}-3^{200}}{10^{100}+3}=\left(10^{100}\right)^{199}-\left(10^{100}\right)^{198} 3+\cdots+10^{100} 3^{198}-3^{199}
\]
is an integer. Moreover, \( I=\left\lfloor\frac{10^{20000}}{10^{100}+3}\right\rfloor \), since
\[
\frac{3^{200}}{10^{100}+3}=\frac{9^{100}}{10^{100}+3}<1
\]

By (1),
\[
I \equiv-3^{199} \equiv-3^{3}(81)^{49} \equiv-27 \equiv 3 \quad(\bmod 10)
\]
so the units digit of \( I \) is 3 .

descriptive_long.question:
What is the units (i.e., rightmost) digit of
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{10^{20000}}{10^{100}+3}\right\rfloor ?
\]
%Here $\lfloor x \rfloor$ is the greatest integer less than or equal to
%x$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Taking \( basepower=10^{100} \) and \( subtrahend=-3 \) in the factorization
\[
basepower^{200}-subtrahend^{200}=(basepower-subtrahend)\left(basepower^{199}+basepower^{198} subtrahend+\cdots+basepower subtrahend^{198}+subtrahend^{199}\right)
\]
shows that the number
\[
quotient=\frac{10^{20000}-3^{200}}{10^{100}+3}=\left(10^{100}\right)^{199}-\left(10^{100}\right)^{198} 3+\cdots+10^{100} 3^{198}-3^{199}
\]
is an integer. Moreover, \( quotient=\left\lfloor\frac{10^{20000}}{10^{100}+3}\right\rfloor \), since
\[
\frac{3^{200}}{10^{100}+3}=\frac{9^{100}}{10^{100}+3}<1
\]

By (1),
\[
quotient \equiv-3^{199} \equiv-3^{3}(81)^{49} \equiv-27 \equiv 3 \quad(\bmod 10)
\]
so the units digit of \( quotient \) is 3 .

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
What is the units (i.e., rightmost) digit of
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{10^{20000}}{10^{100}+3}\right\rfloor ?
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Taking \( lighthouse=10^{100} \) and \( seashells=-3 \) in the factorization
\[
lighthouse^{200}-seashells^{200}=(lighthouse-seashells)\left(lighthouse^{199}+lighthouse^{198} seashells+\cdots+lighthouse seashells^{198}+seashells^{199}\right)
\]
shows that the number
\[
telescope=\frac{10^{20000}-3^{200}}{10^{100}+3}=\left(10^{100}\right)^{199}-\left(10^{100}\right)^{198} 3+\cdots+10^{100} 3^{198}-3^{199}
\]
is an integer. Moreover, \( telescope=\left\lfloor\frac{10^{20000}}{10^{100}+3}\right\rfloor \), since
\[
\frac{3^{200}}{10^{100}+3}=\frac{9^{100}}{10^{100}+3}<1
\]

By (1),
\[
telescope \equiv-3^{199} \equiv-3^{3}(81)^{49} \equiv-27 \equiv 3 \quad(\bmod 10)
\]
so the units digit of \( telescope \) is 3 .

garbled_string.question:
What is the units (i.e., rightmost) digit of
\[
\left\lfloor \frac{10^{20000}}{10^{100}+3}\right\rfloor ?
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Taking \( hvjksqle=10^{100} \) and \( rmpzcloa=-3 \) in the factorization
\[
hvjksqle^{200}-rmpzcloa^{200}=(hvjksqle-rmpzcloa)\left(hvjksqle^{199}+hvjksqle^{198} rmpzcloa+\cdots+hvjksqle rmpzcloa^{198}+rmpzcloa^{199}\right)
\]
shows that the number
\[
ndbvwjqe=\frac{10^{20000}-3^{200}}{10^{100}+3}=\left(10^{100}\right)^{199}-\left(10^{100}\right)^{198} 3+\cdots+10^{100} 3^{198}-3^{199}
\]
is an integer. Moreover, \( ndbvwjqe=\left\lfloor\frac{10^{20000}}{10^{100}+3}\right\rfloor \), since
\[
\frac{3^{200}}{10^{100}+3}=\frac{9^{100}}{10^{100}+3}<1
\]

By (1),
\[
ndbvwjqe \equiv-3^{199} \equiv-3^{3}(81)^{49} \equiv-27 \equiv 3 \quad(\bmod 10)
\]
so the units digit of \( ndbvwjqe \) is 3 .

kernel_variant.question:
Determine the last five (base-10) digits of the integer  

\[
N=\frac{10^{15000}-7^{300}}{\,10^{100}+7\!\cdot10^{50}+49 } .
\]

(The numerator is chosen so that the quotient is an integer.)

------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher-degree divisor: the denominator is quadratic in \(10^{50}\), not linear in a power of 10.  One must recognize the factor \(x^{2}+7x+49\) inside \(x^{3}-7^{3}\) to prove integrality of the quotient.

•  Much larger exponents: exponents reach \(15\,000\) (vs. \(1150\) in the kernel variant), forcing careful modular-exponent computations.

•  Five-digit residue: obtaining the last \(5\) digits (as opposed to the last \(1\)) requires working modulo \(10^{5}=2^{5}5^{5}\).  This necessitates systematic use of the Chinese Remainder Theorem, lifting inverses simultaneously modulo \(32\) and \(3125\), and managing non-trivial carries.

•  Nontrivial modular power: \(7^{300}\) modulo \(3125\) (order \(2500\)) does not collapse under a tiny cycle; one must compute it deliberately (or use group-theoretic insights), unlike the simple \(\bmod 10\) case in the original.

•  Multiple interacting ideas: algebraic factorization, size estimates for the floor, modular inverses, high-precision modular exponentiation, and CRT all interact, demanding several layers of reasoning rather than a single trick.

These additions make the enhanced variant significantly more sophisticated and labor-intensive than both the original and the current kernel problems.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Determine the last five (base-10) digits of the integer  

\[
N=\frac{10^{15000}-7^{300}}{\,10^{100}+7\!\cdot10^{50}+49 } .
\]

(The numerator is chosen so that the quotient is an integer.)

------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher-degree divisor: the denominator is quadratic in \(10^{50}\), not linear in a power of 10.  One must recognize the factor \(x^{2}+7x+49\) inside \(x^{3}-7^{3}\) to prove integrality of the quotient.

•  Much larger exponents: exponents reach \(15\,000\) (vs. \(1150\) in the kernel variant), forcing careful modular-exponent computations.

•  Five-digit residue: obtaining the last \(5\) digits (as opposed to the last \(1\)) requires working modulo \(10^{5}=2^{5}5^{5}\).  This necessitates systematic use of the Chinese Remainder Theorem, lifting inverses simultaneously modulo \(32\) and \(3125\), and managing non-trivial carries.

•  Nontrivial modular power: \(7^{300}\) modulo \(3125\) (order \(2500\)) does not collapse under a tiny cycle; one must compute it deliberately (or use group-theoretic insights), unlike the simple \(\bmod 10\) case in the original.

•  Multiple interacting ideas: algebraic factorization, size estimates for the floor, modular inverses, high-precision modular exponentiation, and CRT all interact, demanding several layers of reasoning rather than a single trick.

These additions make the enhanced variant significantly more sophisticated and labor-intensive than both the original and the current kernel problems.
